Facts.text: A solar eclipse is recorded by a monastic chronicler in Ireland
Kingdom of the West Franks crowns Robert I as King of West Francia, after deposing Charles the Simple (Charles III)
Former Byzantine Emperor Romanos IV Diogenes blinded by rivals and exiled
Raymond of Antioch is defeated and killed at the Battle of Inab in modern-day Syria by the Zengid army of the atabeg Nur al-Din Zengi
Sverre [Sigurdsson] is crowned King of Norway after years of dispute
Holy Roman Emperor Henry VII crowned
French raid on Rye, England
The Russians, led by Prince Trubetskoy are defeated by the Ukrainian armies of Ivan Vyhovsky in the Battle of Konotop
Dutch fleet attacks French grain transports
New Governor Charles de la Ralière Des Herbiers arrives at Isle Royale (Cape Breton Island).
515 prominent Filipinos baptized as Catholic
German medieval heroic poem "Nibelungenlied" (the Song of the Nibelungs) rediscovered in Hohenems Castle Library in Vorarlberg, Austria by Jacob Hermann Oreit
British pass Townshend Revenue Act levying taxes on America, helping to intensify opposition to British rule
Mission Dolores founded at San Francisco Bay by Lieutenant José Joaquin Moraga and Francisco Palóu
Alexander Macdonell and over five hundred Roman Catholic highlanders leave Scotland to settle in Glengarry County, Ontario
Free mason lodge establishes in Alkmaar
Orkney woman Isobel Gunn sets sail for Prince Rupert's Land disguised as a man to work for the Hudson Bay Company - her true identity only revealed when she gives birth December 1807
Autocephaly Officially Granted by the Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople to The Church of Greece.
Netherlands allows corporal punishment
Battle at Chinhat (Indies rebel under Barkat Ahmed beat British)
Great fire in London docks
Treaty of Algun: China cedes north bank of Amur River to Russia
Day 5 of 7 day Battle of Savage's Station, Virginia
Battle at Westminster or Corbitt's Charge, Maryland: small unit of Delaware cavalry charge Confederate cavalry numbering nearly 6,000, crucially delaying their arrival at Gettysburg
Confederate General Robert Lee orders his forces to concentrate near Gettysburg, Pennsylvania
Very 1st First National Bank opens in Davenport, Iowa
Canada's worst railway accident: Grand Trunk Railway train crashes onto a barge on the Richelieu River killing 100 people
Samuel Crowther, bishop of Niger, becomes the first Black bishop of the Church of England
Pope Pius IX declares Gorcumse holy martyrs

Greek politician Charilaos Trikoupis publishes a manifesto in the Athens daily Kairoi entitled "Who's to Blame?" in which he lays out his complaints against King George. He is elected Prime Minister of Greece the next year.
France annexes Otaheite (Tahiti)
First known recording of classical music is made, Handel's "Israel in Egypt" on wax cylinder
Street railway in Ottawa, Ontario, Canada, commences operation
US National Forest Service organized
Doukhobors burn their weapons as a protest against conscription by the Tsarist Russian government.
Chicago Colts establish MLB record for most runs scored in a game by one team as they maul Louisville Colonels, 36 - 7 at the West Side Grounds, Chicago
Brazo River in Texas floods 12 miles wide causing $10 million damage
The Imperial Chinese Court issues what is essentially a declaration of war against foreigners in China, blaming them for hostilities and giving license to the Boxers for even greater ferocity
British government protests against abuses in Belgian Congo
Two prehistoric bones are found in Weerdingerveen, Drenthe in the Netherlands
US Congress pass the Hepburn Act, permitting the regulation of rates charged by railroads, pipelines, and terminals engaged in interstate commerce
Freiherr Gautsch von Frankenthurn becomes Minister-President of Austria
An attack by Bulgarian General Michael Savov on Greek and Serbian positions causes the start of the Second Balkan War
A provisional government opposed to the Bolsheviks establishes itself at Vladivostok, the Russian port on the Sea of Japan
France grants 1 km² at Vimy Ridge "freely, and for all time, to the Government of Canada, the free use of the land exempt from all taxes."
Canada House opens in London, England
Carter G. Woodson wins Springarn Medal for research into Black history
1st flight from West Coast arrives in Hawaii
First test of Wallace Turnbull's controllable pitch propeller
The Outerbridge Crossing and Goethals Bridge in Staten Island, New York both opened
31.1°C (88°F) highest temperature on this day in De Bilt, Netherlands
First high-speed jet wind tunnel is completed at Langley Field, Virginia
Mexico & Vatican sign Concord
109°F (43°C), Monticello, Florida (state record, until broken in 2015)
USSR & China sign non-aggression treaty
LPGA Western Open Women's Golf, Sunset Ridge CC: Opal Hill scores an easy, 9 & 7 win over Mrs. S.L. Reinhart to claim her first of 2 consecutive major titles
NY's Empire State Building broadcasts high-definition TV with 343 lines
PopePius XIencyclical to US bishops "On motion pictures"
4th Dutch government of Prime Minister Hendrikus Colijn falls
Dixie Clipper completes 1st commercial plane flight to Europe
Anjer (Carnation) Day-anniversary of Prince Bernhard
Batman Comics, mobsters rub out a circus highwire team known as the Flying Graysons, leaving their son Dick (Robin) an orphan
US passes Alien Registration Act requiring Aliens to register
6,000 Jews are murdered in a pogrom in Bucharest, Romania
DiMaggio extends hitting streak to 42 breaking Sisler's record
Germany begins withdrawing U-boats from North Atlantic in anticipation of the Allied invasion of Europe
US forces landed at Nassau Bay, near Salamaua, New Guinea
French Nazi collaboratorPaul Touviershoots 7 Jews dead
German counterattack at Caen
Soviet Armies join in Bobroesjk
US 7th army corps conquers Cherbourg, France
20.6 cm rainfall at Litchville North Dakota (state record)
Ruthenia, formerly in Czechoslovakia, annexed by Soviet Union, becomes Ukrainian SSR
Black Sabbath as the British arrest 2,700 Jews in Palestine as alleged terrorists
British mandatory government of Palestine arrests 100 leaders of Yishnuv
Yanks beat Senators 3-1 starting a 19 game winning streak
US troops withdraw from Korea after WW II
Striker Joe Gaetjens heads US to an upset, 1-0 win over England in a FIFA World Cup group match in Belo Horizonte, Brazil; Americans next win over England: 1994
USS Oriskany becomes 1st aircraft carrier to sail around Cape Horn
XETV TV channel 6 in Tijuana-San Diego, CA (IND) begins broadcasting
Argentine state of siege ends
American Charles Dumas records first high jump over 7' (2.13m) during US Olympic Trials at Los Angeles, California
KYA-AM in San Francisco changes call letters to KDBQ (for 2 weeks)
Launch of Transit 4a, with 1st nuclear power supply (SNAP-3)
The International Labour Organisation (ILO) calls for South Africa's withdrawal at the Geneva Conference in protest of the racial policies of the South African government
First flight of the Vickers (British Aerospace) VC-10 long-range airliner
Frank Howard hits the 5,000th Dodger home run
Beatles' 1st song "From Me to You" hits UK charts
SVB, Students Unions, established under Barrel Regtien
USAF Capt Joseph Henry Engle reaches 85,530 m in X-15
USAF test pilot Joe Engle in X-15 reaches altitude of 53.1 mi (85.5 km) and top speed of 3,432 mph (5,523 km/h) (Mach 4.94)
KBSC (now KVEA) TV channel 52 in Corona-Los Angeles, CA begins
US planes bomb North Vietnam's capital, Hanoi, and the port city of Haiphong for the first time in the Vietnam War
Israel removes barricades, re-unifying Jerusalem
1st Jewish worship service at the White House
US ends 2 month military offensive into Cambodia
Supreme Court rules (5-4) that death penalty is cruel & unusual
USSR launches Prognoz 2 into earth orbit (549/200,000 km)
Military coup in Ethiopia
8.10" (20.57 cm) of rainfall, Litchville No Dakota (state 24-hr rec)
Seychelles gains independence from the United Kingdom
South Africa opposition party, the New Republic Party (NRP), is formed after the integration of the United Party (UP) and Democratic Party (DP)
Supreme Court rules out death penalty for rapists of adults
Mascot San Diego Chicken is reborn at Jack Murphy Stadium
Bomb attack on headquarters of Islamic Party in Tehran, 72 killed
US Voting Rights Act of 1965 extended
Challenger flies back to Kennedy Space Center via Kelly Air Force Base
USSR offers to start talking about banning SDI
NASA launches Intelsat VA F-11
STS 51-F vehicle moves to launch pad
FIFA World Cup Final, Estadio Azteca, Mexico City: Argentina beats West Germany, 3-2 in front of 114,600
Moses Mayekiso, former General Secretary of Metal and Allied Workers Union (MAWU) and member of South African Communist Party (SACP), detained for a second time, spends months in solitary confinement
MLB Philadelphia Phillies relief pitcher Steve Bedrosian is 1st to record 12 saves in 12 attempts

South Africa's National Party adopts five year programme of its objectives, including a political "reform" plan to give Black majority role in national and local government; ANC responds it would consider only a one-man, one-vote system
NY Mets tie their team career high 11 game winning streak
World's first female diocesan Anglican bishop, Dr Penny Jamieson is appointed in Dunedin, New Zealand
6.0 earthquake hits southern Calif
2 earthquakes including 7.4 hits southern California
Algerian head of state, Mohamed Boudiaf, is assassinated by military officers during a public speech at the opening of a cultural center in Annaba
Oakland Athletics' Dennis Eckersley sets record of 26th straight saves for the season
128°F (53°C) Lake Havasu City, Arizona (state record, breaking previous set in 1905)
NBA Draft: Purdue small forward Glenn Robinson first pick by Milwaukee Bucks
Socialist, Tomiichi Murayama, elected premier of Japan
US reopens Guantanamo Naval Base to process refugees
Memphis Mad Dogs 1st CFL game (vs Calgary Stampeders)
Andrea Leah Plummer, of Tennessee, crowned 39th America's Junior Miss
Superman's Action Comic #1 (1938) auctioned at Sotheby at $61,900
Progress M-35 Soyuz Launch (Russia)
Tyrenda Williams, 18, of Alabama, crowned 40th America's Junior Miss
Naval clashes between South and North Korea lead to the death of six South Korean sailors and the sinking of a North Korean vessel
Two car bombs are found in the heart of London at Picadilly Circus.
Thomas Beatie, the world's first pregnant man, gives birth to a daughter
15,000 Japanese anti-nuclear protesters blockade the Japanese Prime Minister's office in Tokyo
16 Naxalite Maoist insurgents in India are killed by police
Three bombs in Balid, Iraq, kill 6 people and injure 45
Beijing Times reports 30% of the Great Wall of China has disappeared due to natural forces and stealing of bricks
Martyna Majok's play "Cost of Living" premieres at the Williamstown Theater Festival (Pulitzer Prize for Drama 2018)
US Defense Secretary Ashton B. Carter lifts Pentagon's ban on transgender people serving in the US armed forces
Battle for Mosul: Iraqi forces retake destroyed Great Mosque of al-Nuri from Islamic State - symbolic site where their leader declared a "caliphate"
Mihai Tudose confirmed as Romanian Prime Minister, by parliament, succeeding Sorin Grindeanu
Vatican treasurer Cardinal George Pell charged with historic sexual offenses in Victoria, Australia
Golden State Killer and former police officer Joseph DeAngelo Jr pleads guilty to 12 murders and dozens of rapes
US Supreme Court rules 5-4 that abortion restrictions in Louisiana unconstitutional, striking down a 2014 state law
Lytton, British Columbia, records Canada's highest-ever temperature of 49.6C (121.3F), before being destroyed by a wildfire the next day
World's first known plague victim identified in the remains of a 5,000-year-old hunter-gatherer in Latvia
Biggest trial in modern French history for November 2015 Paris Attacks, convicts Salah Abdeslam, and 19 others, of terrorism and murder charges, sentences him to a rare 30-year prison term
Earth records its shortest day ever, 1.59 milliseconds faster than a standard day
Latest US murder clearance rates show that half of all murders are left unsolved, less likely to be solved if victim is Black or Hispanic
British court rules government plan to deport asylum seekers to Rwanda as a deterrent is illegal, as it could not be considered a third safe country
US Supreme court rules 6-3 that college race-based admission programs used to increase diversity are illegal under the Equal Protection Clause of the 14th Amendment
ICC Men's Cricket T20 World Cup, Bridgetown, Barbados: India beats South Africa by 7 runs, Hardik Pandya 3/20; Player of the Series: Jasprit Bumrah (India fast bowler 15 wickets)
Iranian presidential snap-election heads to a run-off after leading candidates reformer Masoud Pezeshkian and hardliner Saeed Jalili fail to win a majority
Holy Roman EmperorCharles Vand PopeClement VIIsign the Treaty of Barcelona to bring peace to Italy and to repel Turkish advances
French explorerJacques Cartieris the first European to discover Prince Edward Islands, Canada
UK parliament passes The Act of Attainder over former Chancellor of the Exchequer of EnglandThomas Cromwell, indicting him as a heretic
Italian physician and mathematicianGirolamo Cardanoarrives in Edinburgh, Scotland to treat John Hamilton, Archbishop of St Andrews for over two thousand gold crowns
Sofia Alekseyevna names herself regent of Russia for her brothers Ivan V and half-brotherPeter I
Virginia state constitution adopted andPatrick Henrymade governor
British ex-Prime Minister SirRobert Peelfalls off his horse; dies three days later
George Armstrong Custer, aged 23, appointed Union Brigadier General
PopeLeo XIIIpublishes encyclical Diuturnum illud
Jina Guseva attempts to assassinateGrigori Rasputinat his home town in Siberia
Arthur Meighenreturns to office as Prime Minister of Canada
PopePius XIcanonizes Noël Chabanel, French Jesuit, missionary to the Hurons and one of the eight Canadian Martyrs at the St. Peter's Basilica in Vatican City
PopePius XIpublishes encyclical on Nun abbiamo bisogno (We do not need fascism andMussolini)
Italian boxerPrimo CarneraKOs American defending champion Jack Sharkey in round 6 at Madison Square Garden, NYC to become third European to win the lineal world heavyweight title
George M. Cohanis the first artist to be presented with a US Congressional Gold Medal, by Franklin D. Roosevelt (for raising war morale)
Ford introduces the revolutionary Ford-Ferguson 9N tractor incorporatingHarry Ferguson's three-point hitch system
PopePius XIIencyclical Mystic Corporis (mystic body of Christ)
German generalsRommel& von Rundstedt travel to Berchtesgaden
US Atomic Energy Commission votes to revoke Dr.Robert Oppenheimer's security clearance, effectively ending his career as a nuclear physicist
Dutch Prime Minister Willem Drees refuses resignation ofQueen Juliana(Greet Hofmans)
US Federal Interstate Highway System Act signed by PresidentDwight D. Eisenhowerat Walter Reed Army Medical Center in Bethesda. Maryland
Malenkov,Molotov, Kaganowitsj and Sjepilov leave USSR communist party
FIFA World Cup Final, Råsunda Stadium, Stockholm, Sweden: Vavá and Pelé each score two goals as Brazil defeats Sweden 5-2
PopeJohn XXIIIpublishes his 1st encyclical "On truth, unity, & peace, in charity"

MLB San Francisco Giants outfielderWillie Maysbecomes the 4th player in MLB history with 3 or more home runs twice in one season with a 10th inning blast in Giants 8-7 win over the Phillies in Philadelphia
Civil Rights Act of 1964passes the US Senate after a 60-working-day filibuster by Southern senators
NBC approvesGene Roddenberry's script for the pilot episode of "Star Trek" titled "The Cage"
Keith Richardsis sentenced to 1 year in jail on drugs charge
"Tip-Toe Thru' The Tulips With Me" byTiny Timpeaks at #17
On 'Billy Williams Day' at Wrigley Field, Chicago, the Cubs outfielder passesStan Musial's NL record for consecutive MLB games played (896) as Chicago sweeps St. Louis Cardinals, 3-1 and 12-1
Rolling StonesMick JaggerandKeith Richardsare sentenced for a drug offense
Future Hall of Fame outfielderWillie Stargellhits his 400th career HR as his Pittsburgh Pirates rout the St Louis Cardinals, 9-1 at Busch Stadium
US Vice PresidentWalter Mondalebegins trip to Middle East
Hu YaobangsucceedsHua Guofengas Chairman of the Central Committee of the Communist Party of China
40-year-old Puerto Rican jockeyÁngel Cordero Jr.wins his 5,000th race with a victory in the last race at Belmont; 4th jockey to achieve milestone
Montreal Expos infielderPete Roseplays in record 3,309th MLB game, surpassingCarl Yastrzemskiof the Boston Red Sox
PitcherOrel Hershiserbegins string of making every scheduled start until 1990 for Los Angeles Dodgers
Boston Red Sox trade forTom Seaver
Richard Bransonaboard Virgin Atlantic Challenger II reaches Bishop Rock, Isles of Scilly, fastest crossing of Atlantic
Sparky Andersonis 1st to win 600 games as manager in both leagues
New York Yankees blow 11-4 lead but trailing 14-11Dave Winfield's 8th inning grand slammer beats Toronto 15-14;Don Mattinglyalso grand slams
Vigdís Finnbogadóttiris elected the 4th President of Iceland becoming the world's 1st democratically elected female President
A's Dave Stewart no-hits Blue Jays & Dodger'sFernando Valenzuelano-hits St Louis 6-0, 1st time no-hitters in both leagues
Marla Maplesfather sues National Enquirer for $12M
NHL arbitrator decidesEric Lindrosgoes to Philadelphia Flyers instead of New York Rangers, after Quebec traded him twice; Quebec received 6 player, two draft picks and $15M
George Foremanloses IBF boxing title for refusing to a rematch bout against Axel Schulz

Eminem's mother goes to court claiming defamation of character in a $10 million civil suit, after taking exception to the line "My mother smokes more dope than I do" from her son's single 'My Name Is'
"A.I. Artificial Intelligence", directed bySteven Spielbergand starringHaley Joel Osment,Jude Lawand Frances O'Connor, is released
Romanian gymnastNadia Comăneci(39) becomes a naturalized US citizen at the Oklahoma City, Oklahoma federal courthouse
US Vice PresidentDick Cheney, serves as Acting President for two and a half hours, while PresidentGeorge W. Bushundergoes a colonoscopy procedure.
Diana DeGarmoreleases her single, "Dreams"
Hamdan v. Rumsfeld: The U.S. Supreme Court rules that PresidentGeorge W. Bush's plan to try Guantanamo Bay detainees in military tribunals violates U.S. and international law.
Irish singer and songwriterEnyareceives an honorary doctorate from the National University of Ireland, Galway
FinancierBernie Madoffsentenced to 150 years in US maximum prison, for conducting a massive Ponzi scheme
"Transformers: Dark of the Moon," directed byMichael Bay, is released and ultimately grosses $1.123 billion globally
Islamic State leaderAbu Bakr al-Baghdadiannounces establishment of worldwide "caliphate" at the Great Mosque of al-Nuri in Mosul, Iraq
Stacy Lewiswins the Walmart NW Arkansas Championship
Kylie JennerandKendall Jennerapologize and remove t-shirts from sale featuring Tupac Shakur and others, after accused of exploiting their legacies
Drakereleases his fifth studio album "Scorpion", a double album with 25 tracks
Former South African PresidentJacob Zumasentenced to 15 months in prison on contempt of court charges
SingerR. Kellysentenced to 30 years in jail on nine counts of sex trafficking in Brooklyn, New York
Shakespeare's Globe Theatre in London burns down during a performance of "Henry VIII"
Yusuf I [al-Muayyad billah], ruler of Granada (1333-54), born in The Alhambra, Granada (d. 1354)
Murad I, Sultan of the Ottoman Empire (1362-89), born in Bursa, Ottoman Beylik (d. 1389)
John II of Aragon, King of Aragon (1458-79), King of Navarre (1425-79) and King of Sicily (1458-68), born in Medina del Campo, Castile (d. 1479)
Maria of Aragon, queen of Portugal (1500-17), born in Córdoba, Kingdom of Castile (d. 1517)
Rembert Dodoens [Rembert Van Joenckema], Flemish physician and botanist, born in Mechelen, Spanish Netherlands (d. 1585)
Go-Mizunoo [Kotohito], 108th Emperor of Japan (1611-29) and first of the Edo period, born in Kyōto, Japan (d. 1680)
Masaniello [Tommaso Aniello], Italian fisherman and leader of the 1647 Naples revolt against the rule of Habsburg Spain, born in Naples, Kingdom of Naples, Spanish Empire (now Italy)  (d. 1647)
Constantin Reindl, German composer, born in Jettenhofen, Electorate of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1799)
August Alexander Klengel, German pianist and composer, born in Dresden, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1852)
Giacomo Leopardi, Italian poet and philosopher (The Poems of Leopardi), born in Recanati, Italy (d. 1837)
John Newton Brown, American publisher and Baptist teacher, born in New London, Connecticut (d. 1868)
Hiram Powers, American sculptor (Greek Slave), born in Woodstock, Vermont (d. 1873)
Jacob van Zuylen van Nijevelt, Dutch Minister of Foreign Affairs (1852-53), born in Dordrecht, Netherlands (d. 1890)
William Thomas Clark, American politician and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Norwalk, Connecticut (d. 1905)
Josef Labor, Austrian composer, born in Hořovice, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1924)
Sergei Witte, 1st Prime Minister of Russia (1905-06), born in Tiflis, Caucasus Viceroyalty, Russian Empire (d. 1915)
Joseph Paul Skelly, Irish-American composer and songwriter ("I Should Say So!"; "Little Darling, Dream of Me"), born in Ireland (d. 1895)
George Washington Goethals, American army general and civil engineer who supervised the construction of the Panama Canal, born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1928)
William James Mayo, American surgeon and co-founder (Mayo Clinic in Minnesota), born in Le Sueur, Minnesota (d. 1939)
James Harvey Robinson, American historian (Ordeal of Civilization), born in Bloomington, Illinois (d. 1936)
Anton Beer-Walbrun, German composer, born in Kohlberg, Kingdom of Württemberg (d. 1929)
Ashutosh Mukherjee, Bengali educator, jurist, barrister and mathematician described as the "Banglar Bagh" (Tiger of Bengal), born in Calcutta, Bengal Presidency, British India (d. 1924)
Pietro Bonfante, Italian lawyer (Roman law) and historian, born in Poggio Mirteto, Italy (d. 1932)
Wilbert Robinson, American Baseball HOF catcher (Philadelphia A's, Baltimore Orioles, St. Louis Cardinals) and manager (Baltimore Orioles, Brooklyn Robins), born in Bolton, Massachusetts (d. 1934)
Shigechiyo Izumi, once thought to be the world's oldest man ever but birth date now disputed (120 y 237 d), born in Isen, Tokunoshima, Satsuma Domain, Japan (d. 1986)
William E. Borah, American lawyer and politician (Republican U.S. Senator from Idaho), born in Fairfield, Illinois (d. 1940)
George Ellery Hale, American astronomer (discovery of magnetic fields in sunspot), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1938)

Joseph Carl Breil, American lyric tenor, and composer - one of the first to write for film (Les amours de la reine Élisabeth; Birth of a Nation; Intolerance), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1926)
Leo Frobenius, German ethnologist and archaeologist (African cultures), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1938)
Georg Göhler, German composer (Rückert), born in Zwickau, German Empire (d. 1954)
Nellie Tayloe Ross, American educator, politician, 1st woman to serve as a governor (Wyoming, 1925-27), and administrator (Director of US Mint, 1933-53), born in Saint Joseph, Missouri (d. 1977)
Ludwig Beck, German general (July 20th plot), born in Biebrich, Germany (d. 1944)
Curt Sachs, German musicologist, born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1959)
Francis Brett Young, British physician and writer (White Ladies), born in Halesowen, England (d. 1954)
André Gailhard, French classical music composer, born in Paris, France (d. 1966)
Virginia Pope, American fashion editor (New York Times), born in Chicago (d. 1978)
George Frederick Boyle, Australian-American composer, born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1948)
James Van Der Zee, African-American photographer, born in Lenox, Massachusetts (d. 1983)
Juliane Gabriels, Belgian physician and art historian who was one of the first female doctors in Belgium (Artus Quellin the Elder, Flemish Sculpture), born in Ghent, Belgium (d. 1958)[1]
Robert Schuman, Luxembourg-born French statesman and Prime Minister of France (1947-48), born in Luxembourg City, Luxembourg (d. 1963)
Joseph "Squizzy" Taylor, Australian gangster, born in Brighton, Victoria (d. 1927)
Willie Macfarlane, Scottish golfer (US Open 1925), born in Aberdeen, Scotland (d. 1961)
Aarre Merikanto, Finnish composer (Lemminkäinen; Juha; Schott Concerto), and educator, born in Helsinki, Grand Duchy of Finland (d. 1958)
Antoon Schweigmann, Dutch religious poet and resistance fighter, born in Leeuwarden, Netherlands (d. 1945)
Harold Gilligan, English cricket batsman (4 Tests; captain 1929-30 Tour of NZ), born in Shamley Green, England (d. 1978)
Irene Browne, British actress (All at Sea, Peg O' My Heart), born in London, England (d. 1965)
Fulgence Charpentier, French Canadian journalist, born in Sainte-Anne-de-Prescott, Ontario, Canada (d. 2001)
Ottmar Gerster, German composer (rector of the Liszt Music Academy in Weimar), born in Braunfels, Germany (d. 1969)
Lester Vail, American actor (Dance Fools Dance, Consolation Marriage), born in Denver, Colorado (d. 1959)
Frieda Inescort, Scottish-American stage and screen actress (Hay Fever; Pride and Prejudice (1940)), born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1976)
Hendrik Diels, Flemish conductor, born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1974)
Nelson Eddy, American baritone (duets with Jeanette MacDonald), born in Providence, Rhode Island (d. 1967)
Carl-Heinz Schroth, German actor and director (Reaching for the Stars), born in Innsbruck, Austria (d. 1989)
Ellen Clara Pollock, British-German actress (Wicked Lady, Horror Hospital), born in Heidelberg, Germany (d. 1997)
Alan Blumlein, English electronics engineer, born in Hampstead, London (d. 1942)
Alphaeus Hamilton Zulu, Anglican bishop of Zululand and Swaziland, born in South Africa (d. 1988)
Herb Vigran, American character actor (The Ed Wynn Show - "Ernie"; Gunsmoke - "Judge Brooker"; The Jack Benny Program), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1986)
Manuel Altolaguirre, Spanish poet and publisher (La lenta libertad), born in Málaga, Spain (d. 1959)
Ivan Chernyakhovsky, Russian general (youngest ever Soviet general of the army), born in Uman, Kiev Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1945)
Paul O'Dwyer, Irish-American politician and President of City Council (D-NYC), born in Bohola, County Mayo, Ireland (d. 1998)
Leroy Anderson, American composer (Sleigh Ride; Syncopated Clock), born in Cambridge, Massachusetts (d. 1975)
Paul Lebeau, Flemish writer (Last Rose, Xanthippe), born in Borgerhout, Flanders, Belgium (d. 1982)
Rene Gerber, Swiss composer, born in Travers, Switzerland (d. 2006)
Burgess Whitehead, American baseball player (New York Giants), born in Tarboro, North Carolina (d. 1993)

Bernard Herrmann [Maximillian Herman], American radio and Academy Award-winning film score composer (Mercury Theatre on the Air; Citizen Kane; Vertigo; Psycho; Taxi Driver), and conductor, born in New York City (d. 1975)
Bernhard, German Prince (Consort to Queen Juliana of Netherlands), born in Jena, Germany (d. 2004)
Émile Peynaud, French oenologist, born in Madiran, Gers, France (d. 2004)
Joan Davis [Josephine Davis], American comic radio and screen actress (I Married Joan; The Rudy Vallee Show), born in Saint Paul, Minnesota (d. 1961)
John Toland, American political writer (Rising Sun, Pulitzer 1971), born in La Crosse, Wisconsin (d. 2004)
José Pablo Moncayo, Mexican composer (Huapango), born in Guadalajara, Mexico (d. 1958)
Christos Papakyriakopoulos, Greek mathematician, born in Chalandri, Athens, Greece (d. 1976)
Ellen Kuzwayo, South African member of parliament (ANC) and noted writer ("Call me Woman"), born in Thaba-Nchu, Orange Free State (d. 2006)
Rafael Kubelik, Czech conductor (Cornelia Farooli), born in Býchory, Bohemia, Austria-Hungary (d. 1996)
David Donaldson, Painter and Limner to Her Majesty the Queen in Scotland, born in Chryston, Lanarkshire, Scotland (d. 1996)
Ruth Warrick, American actress (Citizen Kane; All My Children, 1970-2005 - "Phoebe"), civil rights and political activist, born in Saint Joseph, Missouri (d. 2005)
Jack Harkness, British rose breeder (Harkness Roses), born in England (d. 1994)
Lloyd Richards, Canadian-American theaterical director (Joe Turner’s Come and Gone), born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 2006)
Slim Pickens [Louis Burton Lindley Jr], American rodeo performer and actor (Dr Strangelove; Blazing Saddles), born in Kingsburg, California (d. 1983)
Ray Harryhausen, American movie special effect artist (Clash of Titans), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2013)
Frédéric Dard, French writer (San-Antonio), born in Bourgoin-Jallieu, Isère, France (d. 2000)
Vasko Popa, Yugoslavia-Serbian poet (Heaven is a Side Issue), born in Grebenac, Vojvodina, Yugoslavia (d. 1991)
Chou Wen-chung, Chinese-American composer (Mode of Shang), born in Yantai, Shandong, China (d. 2019)
Ronnie Ronalde [Ronald Charles Waldron], British entertainer and world-famous whistler, born in London (d. 2015)
Ezra Laderman, American composer (Jacob and the Indians), born in New York City (d. 2015)
Flo Sandon's [Mammola Sadon], Italian jazz and pop singer (Sanremo Music Festival, 1953 - "Viale d'autunno"), born in Vicenza, Italy (d. 2006)
Cara Williams, American actress (Pete & Gladys, Cara Williams Show), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2021)
Giorgio Napolitano, Italian politician, President of Italy (2006-15), born in Naples, Kingdom of Italy (d. 2023)
Hale Smith, American classical and jazz pianist, arranger, and composer (Toussaint L'Ouverture; Dialogues and Commentaries), born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 2009)
Jaber Al-Ahmad Al-Sabah, Emir of Kuwait, born in Kuwait City (d. 2006)
Rex Hunt, British diplomat and Governor of the Falkland Islands (1982-1985), born in Redcar, North Yorkshire, England (d. 2012)
